last_result.message: The previous action was a read-only probe, so there is no probe step to undo. `undo_last_step` would undo the most recent committed tactic, not that probe.

options.label: Inspect current goal
options.effect_if_selected: This keeps the current committed proof state and asks the manager for semantic goal context.

options.label: Show rewind choices
options.effect_if_selected: This shows committed tactics you can rewind before; it does not treat the read-only probe as an undo target.

lines: &1 (left ) : {b : bool, i, j : int, r, r' : word} [programs are in sync]
&2 (right) : {b : bool, i, j : int, r, r' : word}

source.label: Parsed from EasyCrypt pretty goal text

navigation.confidence_reason: The current pRHL goal has local branch/suffix surgery signals, but the exact guard or statement order still needs inspection.
navigation.why_now: current pRHL state looks past the main opener: the useful next work is local branch/suffix surgery, not changing proof family.

preconditions: Use only when the visible goal has a concrete guard controlling the local branch.
After the case split, use `rcondt`/`rcondf` for forced branches, `swap` for statement order, indexed `wp` for suffixes, `conseq` for a smaller post, or `rnd{1}`/`rnd{2}` for one-sided samples.
If the guard fact needed for `rcondt`/`rcondf` is not in the precondition, treat that as an invariant repair signal.
Probe the smallest local block; do not fresh-restart just because this surgery is syntax-sensitive.
fast_track_probe.expected_next_shape: Branch-specific pRHL residuals where forced-condition, swap, indexed-wp, one-sided-random, or conseq steps can be checked.

anti_routes.reason: This is a branch-local/suffix alignment problem; a fresh restart throws away useful opener and invariant work.
One side has sampling or instrumentation before the suffix; use `rcond`, `swap`, or one-sided `rnd` before plain `wp`.
`sim` is usually too late/too strong before the guard and suffix shape are aligned.

repair_if_fails.next: inspect tactic_forms for swap, rcondt, rcondf, wp, conseq, rnd, or eager
inspect align or goal_info for statement indexes before retrying
use undo_to_checkpoint {} and choose the checkpoint before the call/loop invariant that should carry the missing guard or size fact
inspect lemma_hints or lookup_symbol for the local size/nth/bad-event lemma
downgrade this surgery route only after a smaller prefix and relevant tactic_forms fail

navigation.confidence_reason: The current view exposes a live call frontier for a pRHL/procedure state.
navigation.why_now: current pRHL/procedure frontier has one-sided_frontier evidence; choose a structural step before local closure.

preconditions: Use only on the current pRHL/procedure frontier.
If the first instructions are not aligned, consume or align setup before branch tactics.
When one side has setup before an aligned branch, prefer indexed `sp i j` from the alignment counts.
Probe first; commit only the accepted structural step or prefix.
fast_track_probe.expected_next_shape: Call preservation obligations, or a concrete invariant gap.

anti_routes.reason: first instructions are left `call` and right `none`, so branch frontiers are not aligned.
Inlining everything erases live call handles before the call route is checked.
Plain `sim.` needs EasyCrypt to infer the equality relation. At a pRHL frontier with visible call/setup structure, first make the relation explicit with call, seq/wp, alignment, or conseq.
anti_routes.usable_when: `sim.` becomes a good short tactic when the frontiers are already synchronized or after `conseq`/call/structural steps expose an explicit equality relation for the remaining instructions.

anti_routes.reason: Procedure/frontier goals usually need structural exposure before pure SMT.

repair_if_fails.next: inspect goal_info or align for first-instruction details
inspect call_subgoals/subgoal_gap; if generated obligations lack facts that should have been preserved, use undo_to_checkpoint {} before the call or loop invariant
lookup_symbol before applying non-trivial call/branch lemmas
downgrade this route and choose another current-view navigation item

moves.title: Concrete tactic candidate
moves.applicability: This tactic is already concrete for the current goal.
moves.effect: Committing this tactic would change the EasyCrypt proof state if EasyCrypt accepts it.
evidence: Daemon probe accepted this tactic and it would leave 1 subgoal(s); commit it if that is the intended decomposition.
EasyCrypt accepted this tactic in a read-only probe on the current goal; committing it would still be a separate proof-state change.
moves.derivation: surfaced from try; not daemon-checked on this goal
moves.guarantee: not verified on the current goal; probe before committing; reversible

moves.title: Proof-route context
moves.guidance: Run `-inv-from-lemma <oracle_name>` to derive the call invariant. Never guess `while (true)` without a variant.
moves.applicability: This tactic shape still has placeholders; replace them with concrete EasyCrypt arguments from the current goal before probing or committing it.
moves.runnable_status: Not yet; replace every placeholder with concrete EasyCrypt terms from the current goal before probing or committing this tactic.
missing_input: Concrete EasyCrypt arguments for every placeholder in the displayed tactic.
how_to_complete: Use the current goal variables, hypotheses, precondition, postcondition, and visible program statements to fill the placeholders.

inspect_if_unsure.why: Shows parsed goal structure and resolved identifiers.

inspect_if_unsure.name: Run
inspect_if_unsure.why: Shows valid argument forms when this tactic has several shapes.

limitations: The displayed tactic is a template; fill every placeholder with current-goal arguments before probing or committing it.

moves.title: Residual closing tactic
moves.applicability: This tactic-shaped option should be checked with a read-only probe before committing it.
moves.effect: This is a read-only probe; it asks EasyCrypt what would happen without changing the committed proof state.
evidence: The pRHL programs are synchronized and no call frontier is live; try the residual logical close before reopening program structure.
moves.derivation: read-only probe surfaced from proof-state analysis
moves.guarantee: read-only probe shape; proof state unchanged; probe then decide; reversible

limitations.detail: Daemon probe rejected this tactic; do not commit it in the current proof state. proof-state analysis phase legality marks this action as avoid: No program frontier remains; solve the residual side condition. Use the current proof-state analysis phase.prefer or ca...

Daemon probe accepted this tactic but predicted no progress; committing would be auto-reverted. proof-state analysis phase legality marks this action as avoid: No wrapper/call site remains to expose. Use the current proof-state analysis phase.prefer or candidate...

probe_alternatives.how_to_use: The read-only probe failed before EasyCrypt could validate this tactic. Do not treat this as evidence that the tactic is invalid; retry after the backend is healthy or choose a commit only if the proof route is otherwise clear.

route_health.message: `sim` is usually useful after branch, sampling, and postcondition alignment. The current frontier still shows unmatched structure.

frontier_alignment.summary: Frontier alignment: setup statements appear before the current frontier; loop guards are part of the current proof shape; sampling steps are visible inside the frontier; call sites are visible at the frontier. Use this as a proof-reader map before choosing while/rnd/seq/call tactics.

first_instruction_alignment.right_first: EasyCrypt marks the programs as synchronized; the right-side call is represented by the single residual program column.
first_instruction_alignment.proof_read: The visible frontier is one-sided; use a one-sided tactic, alignment step, or seq cut before applying a symmetric tactic.

rows.left: 4 setup statement(s): j <- 0; (PIR.s, PIR.s') <- ([], []); PIR.s <- j :: PIR.s; ... (1 more)
rows.right: EasyCrypt marks the programs as synchronized; the right-side setup is represented by the single residual program column.
rows.proof_read: EasyCrypt marks the programs as synchronized here; this setup is shared residual program structure printed in one column, not an unmatched opposite-side obligation.

rows.right: EasyCrypt marks the programs as synchronized; the right-side call is represented by the single residual program column.
rows.proof_read: EasyCrypt marks the programs as synchronized here; this row is shared residual program structure printed in one column. Use it to choose the next local proof step, not as evidence of a missing opposite-side statement.

procedure_navigation.how_to_use: Positions for choosing wp/sp depth, which `if` to rcond, and where the next loop/call frontier is. Facts only — pick the tactic and the branch direction yourself.

left.hint: wp -2 -2 / sp 2 2 absorbs the 2 straight-line statement(s) up to L2 before the next frontier

branch_guards.rcond_forms: rcondt{1} 3 or rcondf{1} 3 (direction needs guard entailment — yours to decide)
rcondt{1} 3 or rcondf{1} 3 (direction needs guard entailment — yours to decide)

Wrapper or frontier exposure can preserve call/resource structure.

selected_handles.title: Concrete tactic candidate
selected_handles.why_relevant: Daemon probe accepted this tactic and it would leave 1 subgoal(s); commit it if that is the intended decomposition.
selected_handles.derivation: surfaced from current-state analysis; not daemon-checked on this goal
selected_handles.guarantee: not verified on the current goal; probe before committing; reversible

selected_handles.title: Proof-route context
selected_handles.why_relevant: Run `-inv-from-lemma <oracle_name>` to derive the call invariant. Never guess `while (true)` without a variant.
selected_handles.runnable_status: Not yet; replace every placeholder with concrete EasyCrypt terms from the current goal before probing or committing this tactic.
missing_input: Concrete EasyCrypt arguments for every placeholder in the displayed tactic.

facts_and_diagnostics.fact_note: Only current-frontier facts are shown here; the full proof context artifact keeps the compiler/internal details.

gap_signal.note: Gap likely — the conclusion depends on PIR.s, but no hypothesis or invariant in the current context constrains this state. If you are building or repairing a call/loop invariant it probably needs a conjunct over these. Inspect `call_invariant_skeleton` to see the invariant shapes already used in this development.

notes.message: One-sided sampling residual: b{1} <$ {0,1}. The postcondition still mentions the sampled value or a quantified witness, so this may be a true losslessness side condition or leftover coupling debt.

limitations: does not prescribe a conseq, sim, wp, or skip script
reports residual program evidence before pure-tail obligations

inspect_lookup_handles.effect: All handles here are read-only manager requests; the committed EasyCrypt proof state stays unchanged.
inspect_lookup_handles.manager_note: Some requests may ask EasyCrypt or name resolution; wait for the manager result before choosing the next proof intent.

ask_manager_for.use_when: The latest transition recorded an error.
ask_manager_for.returns: manager-provided read-only context for this proof route

ask_manager_for.use_when: Need parsed goal shape, resolved names, or KB pattern hints.
The visible frontier contains call sites or named equiv handles may apply.
ask_manager_for.returns: call-ready/oracle-equiv context for the current frontier, plus the BODY of the procedure(s) called here (functor aliases resolved) so you can see which oracles/state your call invariant must preserve

ask_manager_for.use_when: You are at an abstract-adversary `call (_: <inv>)` and want the mechanical glob frame of the invariant before adding your own semantic conjuncts.
ask_manager_for.returns: the mechanical `={glob ...}` frame of the call invariant (shared abstract oracle modules, daemon-confirmed to apply and spawn the obligations). When a named coupling predicate fits the in-scope state it ALSO surfaces a field-threaded carrier (`inv … s1{1} s2{2}`), plus candidate inductive side-conditions (`dom ⊆ used-set`) for any random oracle the call queries. All revisable starting points — confirm the coupling and add/adjust the semantic conjuncts (key/state correspondences, set-membership).

ask_manager_for.use_when: You are considering `call (_: Inv)` and already have a concrete invariant expression you want to test first.
ask_manager_for.returns: a read-only preview of the obligations caused by that candidate invariant, including pre/init, call-preservation, oracle-side, postcondition, missing-fact signals, and extra conjuncts that may create avoidable obligations

ask_manager_for.use_when: A tactic has multiple EasyCrypt argument forms.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid tactic forms and common traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: The frontier may need indexed `sp i j` before branch or call tactics.
Mid-proof pRHL suffix surgery may need indexed `wp`.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid indexed `wp` forms and suffix-alignment traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: Statement order may need a small `swap` before `sp`, `wp`, or `sim`.
A guarded branch may need `rcondt` after a case split or invariant fact.
A guarded branch may need `rcondf` after a case split or invariant fact.
A suffix proof may need `conseq` to weaken to a smaller relation before `sim`.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid `conseq` forms and pRHL memory-annotation traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: One side may have an extra sample or need an explicit sample coupling.
A known statement-order mismatch may need an eager/lazy transformation.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid `eager` forms and when to prefer smaller surgery

ask_manager_for.use_when: LHS/RHS statement order may need swap/alignment context.
ask_manager_for.returns: swap/alignment context for the visible pRHL frontier
